title: Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives
description: The standard global textbook on derivatives, comprehensively covering futures, options, swaps, credit derivatives, exotic options, interest rate models, and quantitative risk management. In its 11th edition (Pearson, 2021), it remains the primary graduate-level reference in financial engineering and MBA programs worldwide.
keyIdeas: Derivatives function primarily for risk management (hedging) and price discovery (speculation)
The Black-Scholes-Merton no-arbitrage framework provides the foundational pricing machinery for options
Volatility smiles demonstrate the empirical falsity of Black-Scholes's lognormal assumption
Interest rate derivatives require forward-measure and short-rate models that differ fundamentally from equity option models
Credit derivatives and counterparty risk are structural to systemic financial stability, not marginal additions
Model risk — the gap between model assumptions and market reality — is the primary source of derivatives-related disasters
rationale: Selected as Book #1 in the derivatives-and-structured-products category because it is the canonical textbook across MBA and financial engineering programs globally; its 11th edition provides current coverage of SOFR/LIBOR transition and post-2008 regulatory changes, and its Chapter 34 on derivatives mishaps provides the primary empirical grounding for the history of derivatives failure.
subjectAreas: Derivatives instruments: futures, forwards, options, swaps
Options pricing: Black-Scholes-Merton, binomial trees, numerical procedures
Interest rate modeling: Vasicek, CIR, Hull-White, HJM, LMM
Credit risk and credit derivatives: Merton structural models, CDS, CDOs
Risk management: VaR, Expected Shortfall, Greeks, GARCH
Volatility modeling: implied volatility surfaces, smile dynamics, stochastic volatility
Exotic options: barriers, Asians, lookbacks, compound and chooser options
Institutional mechanics: margin, clearinghouses, position limits, exchange conventions
